fingerprint_1.name: H(r) = 8,537 × exp(−r/8,619) is curve-fitted to globe WGS84 distances — the 8,537 km supremum that makes 7.83 Hz mathematically impossible is itself a fit parameter anchored to globe geometry
fingerprint_1.kernel_of_truth: The dome author is correct that a flat-disc geometry with a radius-dependent firmament height H(r) is a mathematically well-defined surface. The exponential form H(r) = H₀·exp(−r/L) is a legitimate two-parameter family and the numerical fit to city-pair distances is not trivially wrong — the exponential beats the elliptical and parabolic alternatives on map error (test_8537.py demonstrates this). The claim "the dome can reproduce approximate globe distances" is true within the fit's tolerance.
fingerprint_1.but_here_is_why_it_fails: The constants 8,537 and 8,619 are NOT derived from any independent dome physics. They are empirically fitted against globe-WGS84 great-circle distances using map_error minimization. The dome repo contains two smoking-gun files: test_8537.py (tries three H(r) families — exponential, ellipse, parabola — all with H₀ = 8,537 km, and minimizes mean error against known city-pair distances) and find_curve.py (explicitly solves "what H(lat) would preserve the WGS84 mapping r = (90−lat) × 111.1 km?" — the 111.1 km/deg being the globe arc length per degree of latitude = πR_Earth/180). Once H(r) is fitted to preserve globe distances, its supremum H(0) = 8,537 km is no longer free. When that same H(r) is plugged into the quarter-wave formula f = c/(4H), the polar frequency is fixed at c/(4·8,537) = 8.77 Hz. The Schumann fundamental 7.83 Hz requires H = c/(4·7.83) = 9,572 km — which exceeds the dome's own fitted supremum by 12%. The dome calibrated H(r) against one globe fingerprint (WGS84 distances) and that very calibration guaranteed it would fail against another globe fingerprint (Schumann resonance). This is the structural reason the "9,572 km" and "9,086 km" heights the author invokes are orphaned constants: they are the heights the quarter-wave and parameterization arguments REQUIRE, but H(r) was pinned to lower values by the distance fit.

globe_value.name: 111.1 km/deg (arc length per degree of latitude on globe)
globe_value.source: WGS84 mean Earth radius; standard geodesy

dome_value.name: Fitted H(r) = 8,537·exp(−r/8,619)
dome_value.derivation: Constants chosen by test_8537.py map-error minimization against globe city-pair distances (e.g., London-NY 5,570 km, Cape Town-BA 6,860 km). find_curve.py explicitly computes H_required(lat) = r·tan(lat) assuming r = (90−lat)·111.1 km — the globe-derived radial mapping.
dome_value.supremum: H(0) = 8,537 km; strictly monotonically decreasing thereafter

quantitative_link.interpretation: The minimum quarter-wave height that produces 7.83 Hz exceeds the dome's fitted supremum by 12%. No averaging scheme over H(r) can reach 9,572 km because E[f(X)] ≤ sup f(X). The supremum is pinned by the distance fit; raising it would break the distance predictions.

code_evidence.file_1_detail: Defines six reference cities with globe lat/lon, three reference distance pairs (Stockholm-Oslo 416 km, London-NYC 5570 km, Cape Town-BA 6860 km — all globe great-circle values), then tests three candidate H(r) families using binary search to find r satisfying r·tan(lat) = H(r). Computes mean error against the globe-derived distances. The file's name "test_8537.py" and its hardcoded H₀ = 8,537 km reveal the constant is treated as a fit parameter under test, not a physical prediction.
code_evidence.file_2_detail: Contains the literal line: "Required H(lat) to maintain perfect WGS84 map radius (r = (90-lat)*111.1)" and iterates latitudes printing H_req = r·tan(lat) for each. This is the definitional statement that H(r) is defined to preserve globe geometry. The 111.1 km/deg is πR_Earth/180° — a pure globe quantity.
code_evidence.verification_method: GitHub API read of john09289/predictions repo, 2026-04-14

fingerprint_1.why_it_matters: The Section already argues (correctly) that 7.83 Hz = c/(2πR_Earth) requires the spherical Schumann formula, and that the author switches formulas silently. The new layer this fingerprint adds: the dome cannot even attempt to save 7.83 Hz with its own quarter-wave formula because the H(r) it needs is locked out by its own distance-calibration fit. The supremum 8,537 km is not a free physical parameter — it is downstream of the WGS84 calibration. This converts the "formula-switching" argument from a rhetorical observation into a structural dilemma: matching Schumann would require H(0) ≈ 9,572 km, which would break the distance fit (test_8537.py shows elliptical and parabolic forms with the same H₀=8,537 test worse, suggesting raising H₀ would also harm distance fit). The dome is trapped: H(0) is constrained by distances from above and by Schumann from below, and the two constraints do not intersect.

defender_rebuttal_and_counter.defender_claim: Every physical theory has fit constants. R_Earth = 6,371 km is also a "fit" to geophysical measurements. The 8,537 km value is no worse.
defender_rebuttal_and_counter.counter: R_Earth is independently measured by multiple uncorrelated methods (Eratosthenes-style shadow geometry, satellite geodesy, seismology, ring-laser interferometry, gravimetry). Its value is determined before any cosmological model is invoked. H(r) = 8,537·exp(−r/8,619) has a single purpose: making flat-disc distances approximate globe distances. test_8537.py makes this explicit — the criterion is map_error vs. known WGS84 city pairs, nothing else. The two constants are categorically different: one is a measurement constraint the dome model must match; the other is a fit parameter the dome model chose to match it.
defender_rebuttal_and_counter.defender_claim_2: Perhaps a different H(r) shape with H₀ ≈ 9,572 km would work for both Schumann and distances.
defender_rebuttal_and_counter.counter_2: test_8537.py tested three shapes (exponential, ellipse, parabola), all with H₀ = 8,537 km, and selected the exponential on map-error grounds. Raising H₀ to 9,572 km would require re-running the fit — but the fit minimum is at 8,537 km for the tested families with the given distance pairs. Any H₀ increase moves the polar apex up but also changes all interior values, because the exponential's scale L is a separate parameter. A thorough sensitivity analysis would be needed to rule this out, but the author never publishes one; the numerical evidence we have is that 8,537 km was chosen because it minimized error, and deviations hurt. The defender must provide the alternative fit and show its map error is comparable — so far no such alternative exists in the repo.

fingerprint_2.name: The averaging domain r ∈ [0, 20,015 km] is itself a globe arc length
fingerprint_2.kernel_of_truth: The section's supplementary averaging calculations (linear radial ≈ 22.6 Hz, area-weighted ≈ 35 Hz) are mathematically correct given the dome's H(r). The author is correct to claim that "some spatial average" is the relevant quantity if one insists on interpreting f = c/(4H) as a cavity mean.
fingerprint_2.but_here_is_why_it_fails: Every one of those averages integrates r from 0 to a = 20,015 km. This integration domain is not neutral — a = πR_Earth (π × 6,371 = 20,015.1 km, match to 0.0006%). On a globe, πR is the distance from the north pole to the south pole along any meridian. When the dome performs a "radial average" from r=0 to r=a, it is mathematically equivalent to averaging along a pole-to-antipode meridian arc on the globe. The integration domain itself encodes globe geometry. The dome pretends this is a free disc parameter, but a = πR_Earth is the azimuthal-equidistant projection identity: the disc radius only equals 20,015 km because the underlying globe has circumference 2πR_Earth = 40,030 km and the dome projection stretches half the globe's surface onto a disc of that radius.

globe_value.name: Pole-to-antipode meridian arc on globe
globe_value.source: AE projection identity — identical fingerprint flagged in WIN-001 and Section 4.5.9 (1.57c ≈ π/2) and WIN-002 globe-fingerprint

dome_value.name: Disc radius a (upper bound of radial integration)

fingerprint_2.why_it_matters: The section's Supplementary paragraph computes three averages of H(r) over r ∈ [0, a]. All three fail to reach 7.83 Hz. A dome defender could say: "Try a different weighting and it might work." The πR fingerprint in the domain itself forecloses this escape: you cannot recover the globe Schumann frequency (c/(2πR)) from a quarter-wave integral c/(4·<H>_w) regardless of the weighting w, because the two formulas have incompatible dimensional structures — the globe formula's 2πR is a circumference (1D path length around a sphere), while the dome's 4·<H> is four times a height (vertical standing wave). Even if you chose a weighting that made <H>_w equal πR_Earth/2 ≈ 10,005 km (which would give f = c/(4·πR/2) = c/(2πR) = 7.83 Hz exactly), you would need H(r) to reach 10,005 km somewhere — and the dome's own fit pins sup H = 8,537 km. Only by treating a as πR_Earth (acknowledging the globe fingerprint) can one even construct a weighting that would give 7.83 Hz — but doing so requires heights the dome cannot provide.

quantitative_link.a_over_pi: 20,015 / π = 6,371.0 km (= R_Earth to better than 0.001%)
quantitative_link.if_average_H_equals_pi_R_over_2: f = c/(4·πR/2) = c/(2πR) = 7.83 Hz exactly (would reproduce the globe Schumann formula)
quantitative_link.H_max_required: 10,005 km (versus dome sup H = 8,537 km — 15% short)

defender_rebuttal_and_counter.defender_claim: 20,015 km is just the disc radius; any similarity to πR_Earth is coincidence — the dome chose a based on map-fit, not on π.
defender_rebuttal_and_counter.counter: The match is to 0.0006%. A parameter-space match at this precision between a "disc radius" and πR_Earth is not coincidence — it is the AE projection identity. This is the same fingerprint Section 4.5.9 flags in the phase-velocity calculation (1.57c = π/2 × c), and the same one Tesla's 0.08484 s reproduces. The "map-fit" to globe distances is exactly what enforces a = πR_Earth: preserving great-circle distances on a flat disc centered at the north pole requires the southern rim to sit at the antipodal distance πR.

suggested_additions.target: SEC-2.1, at the end of the "Supplementary — rigorous averages" paragraph
suggested_additions.text: A structural note: the polar value H(0) = 8,537 km is not a free dome parameter. The repository files test_8537.py and find_curve.py show it is a fit constant chosen to minimize map error against globe-WGS84 city-pair distances — explicitly, find_curve.py prints "Required H(lat) to maintain perfect WGS84 map radius (r = (90−lat)·111.1 km)", tying H(r) directly to the globe's 111.1 km/deg of latitude. To produce 7.83 Hz from f = c/(4H) requires H = 9,572 km, which exceeds the fitted supremum by 12%. The dome cannot raise H₀ to close this gap without breaking its own distance predictions. The 7.83 Hz impossibility is therefore not merely formula-switching — it is a structural consequence of having calibrated H(r) against one globe fingerprint (distances), which locks out a second globe fingerprint (Schumann height).
suggested_additions.rationale: Converts the formula-switching observation into a trap: the dome cannot save 7.83 Hz without breaking its own distance fit.

suggested_additions.target: SEC-2.1, add a short note after the "Supremum impossibility" paragraph
suggested_additions.text: The integration domain r ∈ [0, 20,015 km] is itself a globe quantity: the disc radius a equals π·R_Earth to 0.0006%, i.e., the distance from the north pole to its antipode along a globe meridian. Every radial or area-weighted average over the disc is therefore mathematically an average along a globe arc, which is why only spherical-mode formulas like c/(2πR) reproduce the observed frequency. A quarter-wave formula c/(4H) cannot be rescued by any weighting.
suggested_additions.rationale: Pre-empts the "try a different weighting" defense by noting the domain itself is globe-derived.

files_checked: inject_ai_layer.py (listed, not read — no evidence of Schumann-specific fitting there)
key_findings: test_8537.py: tests three H(r) families (exp, ellipse, parabola) all with H₀=8,537 km; criterion is map_error against globe city-pair distances (416 km, 5570 km, 6860 km — all WGS84 great-circle values)
find_curve.py: literal comment "Required H(lat) to maintain perfect WGS84 map radius (r = (90-lat)*111.1)"; uses the globe arc-length-per-degree 111.1 km to define H requirements
No script computes a Schumann frequency from H(r) — the Schumann claim is never actually derived in the dome code; only the exponential fit to distances is verified
The filename "test_8537.py" itself encodes the constant as a hypothesis under test

newsworthy: Dome firmament height is a curve-fit to globe distances — pinning the supremum 12% below what its own Schumann formula requires
